sections.heading: What is a Service?
sections.content: A Service is an Android application component that runs in the background to perform long-running operations without a user interface. Unlike Activities, Services do not have a visual presence — they run silently behind the scenes handling tasks like playing music, downloading files, syncing data, processing uploads, or maintaining a persistent network connection. Services run on the main thread by default and must manually dispatch work to background threads. Android defines three types of services: started services (run until explicitly stopped), bound services (run while clients are bound to them), and foreground services (started services with a persistent notification that cannot be killed by the system under memory pressure).

rows.lifecycle: Runs until stopSelf() is called or the system kills it under memory pressure
rows.use_case: Fire-and-forget background tasks: uploading a file, processing data

rows.lifecycle: Runs while at least one client is bound; destroyed when all clients unbind
rows.use_case: IPC between components: Activity communicating with a background music player

rows.lifecycle: Runs until explicitly stopped; system cannot kill it; requires a persistent notification
rows.use_case: User-visible ongoing operations: navigation, music playback, location tracking, file download

rows.lifecycle: Processes intents sequentially on a background thread; stops itself when done
rows.use_case: Legacy — replaced by WorkManager or coroutines in a regular Service

sections.heading: When to Use a Service vs Alternatives
items: Use a Foreground Service for operations the user is actively aware of: music playback, navigation, ongoing download
Use WorkManager for deferrable background work that must complete even if the app is killed: sync, cleanup, uploads
Use coroutines in ViewModel/Repository for short-lived background work tied to the app's lifecycle
Use JobScheduler (via WorkManager) for scheduled or constrained background work
Do NOT use a plain started Service for network calls or database operations — it runs on the main thread
Do NOT use a Service when a ViewModel + coroutine will suffice — Services are heavier components
A foreground service is required for any task that must continue when the user navigates away from the app
Android 14+ restricts foreground service types — you must declare the type in the manifest and have a legitimate use case

sections.heading: Service Lifecycle
sections.content: The Service lifecycle differs depending on how it is started. A started service follows the path: onCreate() → onStartCommand() → (running) → onDestroy(). A bound service follows: onCreate() → onBind() → (bound) → onUnbind() → onDestroy(). A service can be both started and bound simultaneously. onCreate() is called once when the service is first created. onStartCommand() is called every time startService() is called and is where started services receive their Intent and begin work. onDestroy() is the last callback — use it to clean up threads, listeners, and resources.

rows.when_called: Called once when the service is first created — before onStartCommand() or onBind()
rows.use_for: One-time initialization: creating threads, initializers, registering receivers

rows.when_called: Called every time a client calls startService() or startForegroundService()
rows.use_for: Receive the Intent and begin background work; return a START_* constant

rows.when_called: Called when a client binds with bindService()
rows.use_for: Return an IBinder to the client for communication; return null for started services

rows.when_called: Called when all clients have unbound
rows.use_for: Clean up bound resources; return true to allow rebinding via onRebind()

rows.when_called: Called when a new client binds after onUnbind() returned true
Called when the service is about to be destroyed — final cleanup opportunity
rows.use_for: Cancel coroutines, unregister receivers, release resources, call stopForeground()

sections.heading: onStartCommand Return Values
items: START_STICKY — service is restarted after being killed; onStartCommand() is called with a null intent; use for services that should always be running (music player)
START_NOT_STICKY — service is NOT restarted after being killed; use for services doing work that can be safely abandoned (upload that can be retried later)
START_REDELIVER_INTENT — service is restarted and the original intent is re-delivered; use for services where completing the specific task is critical (file download)
START_STICKY_COMPATIBILITY — compatibility version of START_STICKY for older API levels
Most foreground services use START_STICKY or START_REDELIVER_INTENT
The startId parameter identifies the specific start request — use stopSelf(startId) to stop after processing that specific request
stopSelf() stops the service immediately regardless of how many times it was started
stopSelf(startId) only stops the service if no newer start requests are pending — safer for sequential processing

sections.heading: Foreground Services
sections.content: A foreground service is a started service that is visible to the user through a persistent, non-dismissable notification in the status bar. Because the user is aware of its operation, Android treats foreground services with higher priority and will not kill them under normal memory pressure conditions. Foreground services are required for any background operation the user is actively engaged with — music playback, navigation, file download progress, fitness tracking, or VoIP calls. Starting with Android 9 (API 28), apps must call startForeground() within 5 seconds of calling startForegroundService() or the system throws an ANR.

sections.heading: Foreground Service Setup Steps
items: Step 1: Declare in AndroidManifest.xml: <service android:name=".MyForegroundService" android:foregroundServiceType="dataSync" />
Step 2: Add permission: <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE" />
Step 3: For Android 14+, add type-specific permission: <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE_DATA_SYNC" />
Step 4: Create a notification channel (required for API 26+) in Application or before showing the notification
Step 5: Build a Notification with NotificationCompat.Builder
Step 6: In onStartCommand(), call startForeground(NOTIFICATION_ID, notification) immediately
Step 7: When work is done, call stopForeground(STOP_FOREGROUND_REMOVE) then stopSelf()
Step 8: Start from app: startForegroundService(Intent(this, MyForegroundService::class.java))

rows.description: Syncing data to or from the network or another device
rows.example: Uploading files, syncing contacts, backup operations
Music player, podcast app, video streaming

rows.description: Accessing device location in the background
rows.example: Navigation, fitness tracking, delivery driver app

rows.description: Accessing the device camera in the background
rows.example: Video call app, continuous scanning app

rows.description: Accessing the device microphone in the background
Accessing health, fitness, or medical sensor data

sections.heading: Creating the Foreground Notification
items: Create a NotificationChannel in Application.onCreate() or before the service starts — required for API 26+
val channel = NotificationChannel(CHANNEL_ID, "Service", NotificationManager.IMPORTANCE_LOW) — use LOW importance to avoid sound on every update
Register channel: (getSystemService(NOTIFICATION_SERVICE) as NotificationManager).createNotificationChannel(channel)
Build notification: NotificationCompat.Builder(this, CHANNEL_ID).setContentTitle("Syncing").setContentText("In progress").setSmallIcon(R.drawable.ic_sync).build()
Add a PendingIntent to open the app when the notification is tapped: .setContentIntent(pendingIntent)
For ongoing progress: .setProgress(max, current, false) — update the notification via NotificationManagerCompat.notify()
Use IMPORTANCE_LOW or IMPORTANCE_MIN for the channel to avoid sound and minimize disruption
The notification is automatically removed when stopForeground(STOP_FOREGROUND_REMOVE) is called

rows.description: Return a Binder subclass that provides a reference to the Service itself — only works within the same app process
rows.example: inner class LocalBinder : Binder() { fun getService() = this@MyService }; override fun onBind(intent: Intent) = binder

rows.description: Use Messenger and Handler to pass Message objects across processes — simpler than AIDL for basic IPC
rows.example: val messenger = Messenger(IncomingHandler()); override fun onBind(intent: Intent) = messenger.binder

rows.description: Android Interface Definition Language — define a typed interface for complex cross-process communication
rows.example: Define .aidl file; implement generated interface; return IBinder in onBind()

sections.heading: Binding to a Service from an Activity
items: Implement ServiceConnection: val connection = object : ServiceConnection { override fun onServiceConnected(name, binder) { myService = (binder as MyService.LocalBinder).getService() }; override fun onServiceDisconnected(name) { myService = null } }
Bind in onStart(): bindService(Intent(this, MyService::class.java), connection, Context.BIND_AUTO_CREATE)
Unbind in onStop(): unbindService(connection) — always unbind to prevent leaks
BIND_AUTO_CREATE — creates the service if it is not already running when binding
The service's onCreate() is called when the first client binds; onDestroy() when the last client unbinds
A service can be simultaneously started and bound — it only destroys when stopped AND all clients unbind
Check the bound state with isBound flag before calling service methods to prevent NullPointerException
For Compose: use DisposableEffect to bind in composition and unbind on dispose

rows.description: Create a CoroutineScope in the service; cancel it in onDestroy()
rows.example: private val serviceScope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO); override fun onDestroy() { serviceScope.cancel() }

rows.description: Launch background work as a coroutine from onStartCommand()
rows.example: override fun onStartCommand(intent: Intent?, flags: Int, startId: Int): Int { serviceScope.launch { doWork(); stopSelf(startId) }; return START_REDELIVER_INTENT }

rows.description: Use SupervisorJob so a failed child coroutine does not cancel all sibling coroutines
Call stopSelf(startId) after the coroutine completes to stop the service cleanly

sections.heading: Declaring a Service in AndroidManifest.xml
items: All services must be declared inside <application> in AndroidManifest.xml
Basic declaration: <service android:name=".MyService" />
Foreground service: <service android:name=".MyForegroundService" android:foregroundServiceType="dataSync|location" />
Multiple foreground types can be combined with pipe: android:foregroundServiceType="mediaPlayback|location"
Private service (cannot be started by other apps): android:exported="false" — this is the default and recommended
Public service for IPC: android:exported="true" with android:permission to restrict access
Add intent filter if starting by implicit intent: <intent-filter><action android:name="com.example.MY_SERVICE_ACTION" /></intent-filter>
Prefer explicit intents when starting services — implicit intents are blocked for services on API 21+

rows.service: Foreground: Yes. Started: Maybe (START_STICKY). Bound: No
Ongoing user-visible tasks: music, navigation, VoIP
rows.workmanager: Deferrable guaranteed tasks: sync, backup, processing
rows.coroutine: Short-lived tasks tied to app UI: fetch data, submit form

sections.heading: Common Service Mistakes to Avoid
items: Performing blocking work on the main thread in onStartCommand() — always launch a coroutine or thread
Forgetting to call startForeground() within 5 seconds of startForegroundService() — causes ANR on API 26+
Not calling stopSelf() when work is complete — service runs forever wasting battery
Not unbinding from a bound service in onStop() — causes a ServiceConnection leak
Starting an unneeded foreground service when WorkManager would suffice — misuse draws Play Store scrutiny
Not creating a NotificationChannel before calling startForeground() on API 26+ — notification is silently ignored
Using an implicit Intent to start a service on API 21+ — throws IllegalArgumentException
Forgetting to cancel the serviceScope CoroutineScope in onDestroy() — coroutines leak beyond service lifetime

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Services are Android's mechanism for running background operations detached from the UI lifecycle. Foreground services are the most commonly needed type — they run persistent, user-visible operations like media playback, navigation, and file transfers with a required notification. Always call startForeground() immediately in onStartCommand(), declare the correct foreground service type in the manifest, run actual work on background threads via a CoroutineScope, and call stopSelf() when the work completes. For work that does not need to be immediately visible to the user, prefer WorkManager. For short-lived work tied to the app lifecycle, prefer coroutines in the ViewModel or Repository without a Service at all.
